The timing of reinitiating immunosuppressive agents for controlling SLE remains obscure, and no definite therapeutic evidence has been reported. Previous literature on VZV infection after renal transplantation has suggested that if immunosuppressive agents cannot be discontinued, such agents should be replaced with cyclosporine, azathioprine and prednisone[22]; mycophenolate mofetil may increase the incidence rate of severe varicella[23]. If high-dose steroids such as mycophenolate mofetil and cyclophosphamide are required because of disease conditions, herpes zoster virus reactivation should be strongly suspected. Specifically, elevated VZV-IgM and VZV-DNA without clinical symptoms indicates subclinical reactivation, and the appearance of herpes zoster or varicella suggests clinical reactivation. In addition, VZV-IgG levels persist during infection[24,25] and are thus of no value for determining the presence or absence of varicella infection. In this case, the patient’s family members refused to undergo VZV-DNA assessment during the recovery period; however, after communication, the patient was given wysolone (20 mg once daily) to treat SLE. Regular follow-up showed no varicella

The optimal timing for reintroducing immunosuppressive agents for managing SLE is still unclear, and there is currently no concrete therapeutic evidence available. Existing literature on VZV infection after kidney transplantation has suggested that if it is not possible to discontinue immunosuppressive agents, alternative medications such as cyclosporine, azathioprine, and prednisone should be considered [22]. However, it should be noted that mycophenolate mofetil may increase the risk of severe varicella [23]. When high doses of steroids like mycophenolate mofetil and cyclophosphamide are necessary due to disease conditions, it is important to be vigilant about possible reactivation of the herpes zoster virus. If there are elevated levels of VZV-IgM and VZV-DNA without any clinical symptoms, it may indicate subclinical reactivation, whereas the presence of herpes zoster or varicella indicates clinical reactivation. It is also worth mentioning that VZV-IgG levels may persist during an infection [24, 25], making them an unreliable indicator for determining the presence or absence of varicella infection. In the case of this patient, their family members declined undergoing VZV-DNA assessment during the recovery period, but after consultation, the patient was prescribed wysolone (20 mg once daily) to treat SLE. Regular follow-up examinations did not reveal the presence of varicella.
rewrite again

The optimal timing for reinitiating immunosuppressive agents to manage SLE remains uncertain, and there is currently no conclusive therapeutic evidence available. Previous research on VZV infection after kidney transplantation suggests that if it is not possible to discontinue immunosuppressive agents, alternative medications like cyclosporine, azathioprine, and prednisone can be considered [22]. However, it is important to note that mycophenolate mofetil may increase the risk of severe varicella [23]. When high doses of steroids such as mycophenolate mofetil and cyclophosphamide are necessary due to disease conditions, careful monitoring for herpes zoster virus reactivation is crucial. Subclinical reactivation may be indicated by elevated levels of VZV-IgM and VZV-DNA without any clinical symptoms, while the development of herpes zoster or varicella points to clinical reactivation. It is also worth mentioning that VZV-IgG levels can persist during an active infection [24, 25], and therefore, they are not reliable for determining the presence or absence of varicella infection. In the case of this patient, their family members declined VZV-DNA assessment during the recovery period. However, after consultation, the patient was prescribed wysolone (20 mg once daily) to treat SLE. Subsequent regular follow-up examinations did not detect the presence of varicella.

Immunocompromised patients with varicella should be informed about the sudden development of abdominal pain and the possibility of visceral disseminated VZV infection, which principally presents as severe abdominal pain, with potential stomach, intestine and spleen involvement[7]. Moreover, abdominal pain may appear several days before skin rashes, resulting in misdiagnosis as other acute abdomen, lupus mesenteric vasculitis or thromboembolic diseases. Visceral disseminated varicella-induced abdominal pain is attributed to the proliferation of VZV in the abdominal cavity and mesenteric ganglia, but the specific mechanism remains unclear[8-11]. Furthermore, VZV infection may be accompanied by vasculitis, presenting as abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhoea and intestinal obstruction when infection occurs in the abdominal blood vessels[12,13].

Patients with compromised immune systems who contract varicella should be made aware of the potential manifestation of visceral disseminated VZV infection, which primarily presents as severe abdominal pain and can involve the stomach, intestines, and spleen [7]. It is important to note that abdominal pain may occur several days before the onset of skin rashes, leading to potential misdiagnosis as other conditions such as acute abdomen, lupus mesenteric vasculitis, or thromboembolic diseases. The exact mechanism behind the development of visceral disseminated varicella-induced abdominal pain is not yet fully understood, although it is thought to be related to the proliferation of VZV in the abdominal cavity and mesenteric ganglia [8-11]. Additionally, VZV infection can result in vasculitis, presenting as abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, and intestinal obstruction if the infection affects the abdominal blood vessels [12,13].

Patients who have compromised immune systems and are diagnosed with varicella should be educated about the potential onset of sudden abdominal pain, as it may indicate visceral disseminated VZV infection. This type of infection primarily manifests as severe abdominal pain and can affect various abdominal organs such as the stomach, intestines, and spleen [7]. It is crucial to note that abdominal pain may precede the appearance of skin rashes by several days, which can lead to misdiagnosis as conditions like acute abdomen, lupus mesenteric vasculitis, or thromboembolic diseases. The specific mechanism behind the development of abdominal pain in visceral disseminated varicella is still unclear, although it is believed to involve the proliferation of VZV in the abdominal cavity and mesenteric ganglia [8-11]. Furthermore, VZV infection can be accompanied by vasculitis, leading to symptoms including abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, and intestinal obstruction if the infection affects the blood vessels in the abdomen [12,13].

Treatment for visceral disseminated VZV infection is administration of sufficient doses of acyclovir until marked reduction of viral load is demonstrable, coupled with reduced dose of immunosuppressive drugs [3, 15]. Doki et al. [7] reported that most survivors were started the administration of acyclovir before or on the same day as the appearance of skin lesions, although non-survivors were started it after the appearance. Therefore, earlier diagnosis and prompt or empiric medical intervention should be needed for the management of this serious complication [16]. In our case, we actually started the administration of acyclovir on the same day as the appearance of skin lesions.

The recommended treatment for visceral disseminated VZV infection involves administering adequate doses of acyclovir until there is a noticeable decrease in viral load, along with a reduction in the dosage of immunosuppressive drugs [3, 15]. Doki et al. [7] observed that most survivors began acyclovir treatment either before or on the same day as the onset of skin lesions, whereas non-survivors started it afterwards. This suggests the importance of early diagnosis and immediate medical intervention for effectively managing this severe complication [16]. In our specific case, the administration of acyclovir commenced on the same day that skin lesions appeared.
rewrite

The treatment approach for visceral disseminated VZV infection involves administering appropriate doses of acyclovir until a noticeable decrease in viral load is observed, in addition to reducing the dosage of immunosuppressive drugs [3, 15]. According to a study by Doki et al. [7], most individuals who recovered from this infection initiated acyclovir treatment either before or on the same day as the onset of skin lesions. Conversely, those who did not survive began treatment after the appearance of skin lesions. These findings underscore the significance of early detection and immediate medical intervention to effectively manage this serious complication [16]. In our case, we promptly initiated acyclovir treatment on the same day that skin lesions appeared.
